speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 25th, 1995. The survivor is Leon Sontag, S-O-N-T-A-G. The interviewer is Elisa Arden, A-R-D-E-N. The interview is taking place in Hallandale, Florida in the Miami district, USA. The language is English. This is roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 25th, 1995. My name is Elisa Arden, A-R-D-E-N. I am conducting an interview with Leon Sontag, S-O-N-T-A-G, who is a survivor. The place is Hallandale, Florida, the United States of America. And the language is English. Could you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Sontag.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-N-T-A-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is-- could you repeat it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the same name I had all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sontag, S-O-N-T-A-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 3rd, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to be 80 in January. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the city and country where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me something about your home town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my hometown was a small town, about 5,000 inhabitants-- 200 Jewish families. And we had-- I had really had the life there, I feel. I was there till I was 16 years old. And my father didn't make a good living there, but he had a tailor shop-- made clothing to order. And we made a poor living. But we had everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father? What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Shea, his first name. And Sontag, like me. And my father was-- now I know he was a young man that time. He wasn't yet 40 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation? What was his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned the tailor trade at home by my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have his own tailor business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't have a special business, what he sold and bought and sold. He just made clothing to order. With the measurement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. And she didn't do any work. But she had plenty work with the children all the time. She had four of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trana-- Trana Sontag. At home, she was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was not very, very religious because he was out of the country. As a young boy he was in Switzerland. Then he came back home. He wasn't very. But my mother was more religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The young ones were twins, Bolek and Alek. And the other one was two years or three years younger than me. He was Shloma. He was killed the first year of the war, right in the beginning, from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much interaction with non-Jewish people in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in my age because I was going public school. And the kids were all together. So when I went out from school-- so I remember the kids used to throw stones on us-- hey, Jew, go to Palestine. [LAUGHS] And I was a kid, I didn't know much. I said, go to you to Palestine. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the school that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was now in Poland in a well to do school to see it. It's the same school, the same everything, but different teachers, different kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join any Zionist groups as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thought I did what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join any Zionist groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, in the later years, yes. Betar-- the later years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your family aware of what was happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not very much. We know about what's happening, but not exact. But nobody could believe things, what's happened. And believe, if they had somebody in the paper. But they didn't believe. They didn't write too much in the Polish paper about what's happened there. But we did know something.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what age did you learn to be a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17. I and my father went there because couldn't make a good living there. We went there. And over there was better-- it was a more industrial city. It was a port city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gdynia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-D-Y-N-I-A. On the ocean, became many tourists with no car in different countries. And there was more prosperity there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember celebrating the Jewish holidays in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother always celebrates. She made big suppers and big things. Always make a real Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I remember all of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Gdynia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in till the war break out. I went away to the army in '38. We came to Gdynia in '33. And I went to the army in '38. And my parents, my family stayed there, in case the war break out, they're on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it that you went into the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? I was called in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the army like in '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not bad. It was-- I-- I liked it in the army. I liked it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What unit were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Infantry-- border infantry. I was on the border of the town between Germany and Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family stay in Gdynia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stayed because the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your family then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sailed around back to Gdynia, lay our boards down, where we growed up, all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your father able to work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He worked there. He worked there in Dabie. I came home in 1940 because I didn't know where they are. But I found out the last months I got a letter from them. They know that I'm in prison. And so I got the letter, and say I was in Dabie back. When I come home in 1940, when they released the Jewish prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that you went into the army in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were in the army then when the war broke out in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was the time I was supposed to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you stationed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Choijnica. It's on the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-O-J-N-I-C-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in '39 when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war break out, we was right in the big fire. We was on the front right away, early in the morning, about 4 o'clock. And the Germans run us over. It's a-- they-- they run us over with a very big army, much stronger and bigger than we were. And we had to draw out form there. We drew out to the middle of town. And it didn't take long. They bombarded the town. Everything was on fire, the whole town. And wherever we went, we saw soldiers of-- our soldiers laying dead. And there was a town full of many Polish Germans, who would say this town belonged to Germany before the First World War. And then when the Germans lost the war, this town went to Poland. All the Germans stayed there. They didn't run away. They become Polish citizen. Everybody thought they're Polish. And all of the sudden they start to shoot on the Polish army, all the citizens in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your unit manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I managed? I managed with the whole army. We drove out from town afternoon. And we rode out about 20, 30 kilometer to the back of town. And there we got help from the Polish army. They came in tanks and thing. And they start to set up a fight there with the Germans. And they all run us over very fast. And there were many killed from our people. And we had to run farther away. And we keep on running. And then we put another front up. And that didn't take more than a half a day. It knocked us out with airplanes and with everything. And were to the end, came close to the river, [NON-ENGLISH]. And there they surround all around. And everybody has to go in prison. So there was no other way out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a group of about 500 people who didn't want to go to prison. We run away to the forest. And everyone was from different companies. So one didn't know the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we run into the forest. And we run away. And on the road we saw how the German coming down, take everybody in prison. And we run-- till the forest end, then we met the Germans again. And we start to set up a fight with them. We was about 500 people. We had machine guns a lot. And we had three canons, artillery. And there was a raid in evening. And the Germans just occupied that little town. So they didn't want to stay there. They were frightened. They come at us again, the Polish armies. They didn't want to fight during the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it figures they are going to take it tomorrow because they went out. They went away. And we went in, occupied back that little town.

speaker: interviewee
text: But all the citizens from the town, they were all Germans. They were frightened. Now the Polish come back. They run away everybody with the German army. And we came in. Nobody was in town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went into the houses, made the first dinner. We didn't eat for three days. [LAUGHS] And then in the evening they caught us. There was 110 people left. And they told us that going to go. It's not far to the river. And nighttime we're going to run over the river. Who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Officers, there was two officers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Polish officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went. We went. When we went into the forest, we arrived when we met the Germans. But we know why the Germans are running away, because we had-- because they shoot two machine guns, we had to get father and father away. So they didn't want to set up a fight during the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we went into the forest, we went for many kilometers. But the people, our people, they had no power anymore to walk. They couldn't walk anymore after they four or five days in the front. They didn't get any food. They didn't get any sleep. They didn't get nothing. So many say-- say, they can't go anymore. I said-- oh, no, it was one officer only. He said to us, I can't take you on my back. Who can't walk? So we're going to stay in the forest. And tomorrow the Germans come. They're going to take him prison. So I said, no. I'm going to walk on all four as well I can. And I'm don't going to give in. I'm not going to give up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went. We were 60 boy here. And we went out in the forest. And in the other forest, the Germans were staying with machine guns. It was nighttime. We didn't even see them. We went straight on the machine guns. And we came about five yards away, they opened fire on us. And we run back to the other forest we came from. On the way, everybody was killed. Only five boys come in-- I and another four.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you five?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went deep into the forest. We was very frightening, tired and frightened. And we went deep in the forest. And we sit down there. And we were so tired, we fall asleep. In the morning, I was the first one to wake up. And I woke them up. I say, let's see what we can do. So there was two guys. I said, I say, I going to go into the people that live there. And we're going to find out where we are at least. They went and didn't come back anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I with another guy, we went. We head down the road how many tanks and automobiles are running. We figured maybe the Polish army runs over the river. If they're on their way, we go to them. If they're Germans, we are prisoners now. What are we going to do? So we went. We went about an hour. We didn't see nobody. When after an hour, we came close to the road, we saw the Germans running there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They saw us-- and we going with machine guns. But I say, no. But we don't going to shoot them. We're going to give up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, we didn't give up yet. We-- from then, we saw what's going on when they got-- we didn't see the Germans for awhile, what's the trunk would close. And the other trunk would came. We run into the forest again. I would not specify how we went. We laid down in a building, where there was German people living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take before the Germans found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took a whole day. This late in the evening we was in the forest. And there the other guy say he don't going to stay anymore, if I want to stay by myself. So he went and gave up. So I went with, went out from the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did the Germans take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we went out from the forest, right away was two policeman, German police. And there was Polish from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us right away, hand up. And that's all And they caught us. And they brought the German army, a couple of soldiers. And they took us to the road. And there on the road came all the transport from the prisoners, ones they caught. You could see them many kilometers far away. So we connect to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked farther. All-- the officers, they took away. In the front were officers, only officers. They took them all on trucks, they took them away. And they took us farther up, just about 12 in the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where did you end up? In 12 in the night, they put on machine guns on us. And they shoot on us. They shoot. And people start to run. They didn't know where they're running. They're dead, here and there, all over. So I and another 20 boys were running.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our gate, we're running in-- in the back yard. And there were stables. We run in the stable. We close ourselves in. And one got a match-- he pu on matches in the German horses from the army staying there. Of course, they're going to come in. We figured they shoot everybody outside. Then they're going to come and kill us. So the war still going on there between Poland and Germany. We were in the middle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, if it's a God someplace, why don't fall in a bomb here. And we should be killed, not waiting till the Germans come and shoot us. And that took about an hour or so. The shooting stopped outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the door opened up. And they come in, about 20 of them with machine guns and bayonets on. And one was an officer with a revolver. And he's like, you Polish pigs, you'll be all killed. And he hollered on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us how long it took you to get your prisoner of war camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] camp, prisoner of war camp, took us I think another 10 days. This was camp-- that was January 4th. January 4th we became imprisoned, or January 5th. And the [NON-ENGLISH], first [NON-ENGLISH] we came to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not yet a camp, the regular camp. It was just Germany brought us in barracks. There was not a real camp. The real camp took another two camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Stalag II B

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long I've been there? Since 1940 in March.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you in the camp? This camp didn't happen much. We was living under a tent, you know, a few hundred people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give a shower, no nothing. So we was living with bugs and everything. And then they decided to liberate us from camps and send us on to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this prisoner of war camp that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalag II B.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did it have another name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The city close to it was Hammerstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to March, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940s, took us out to say we get liberated, to send us home to Poland, we go free. And it was looking suspicious, but nothing we could do. They put us-- they took us in our showers. They could make us clean, and gave us new uniforms, Polish uniforms, brand new ones, everything. And they transported us to another camp not very far away by train. And there, form all the camps in Germany, all the Jewish boys came over there. There were thousands of Jewish boys from the Polish army. And there they made three transports, one to Warsaw. That means that Warsaw was the capital Poland. That time was it to. The Germans gave us small Poland. And one side of Poland they took to Germany to connect it. And one part went to Russia, because Russia fell over, too. And the center was the little Poland. The one was transported to Poland, the one was transport to Russia, and one to transport to Germany. And I was on the side, what belongs to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they registered everybody there the name I think and took a couple days. And they brought down a regular beautiful luxury train, and set us in, and gave us food and everything, and gave us a paper that we are released from prison. So who could think that we're not going home? Nobody could think of it that way. And we came in the middle of the night. They stopped the trains, that everybody should get out. And the Gestapo staying waiting for us. They take us to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: In there they tried the-- the Jewish-- Jewish-- no, how is it said-- the Jewish federation in Lublin that was close to Treblinka. So they should buy out the prisoners. Otherwise, they're going to kill them. And they took them in the town. And they killed a group every day. Finally, we got in connection with the Jewish federation. And they let out-- at first they let out the boys from Lublin, must have had families in Lublin. So they took them out. And there was some space. They took out some others. They took 100 people out in the evening. And I went out with 20 boys, not from Lublin. With the group of Lublin, we came out as free people. And before we went out, they took away our uniforms. We got down and they left us out in the afternoon. And it was snowing [LAUGHS] without shoes, without everything. But I can't complain. Polish people were staying outside on the streets and gave us things to put on. And so-- and they took us to the Jewish Federation, which they got the building there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came there-- so a lady passed. And she says to me. What? You are so down. You are a young person. The war don't going to be forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yes. There where I was in camp was a lot better than here. We're smelling all over, thousands of people laying on the floor, and all levels of space.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was standing on the door, so she say, where are you are from? I say, from Gdnyia. And she said, there as many people in Gdynia from Lublin. And they all came back now. They're all here in Lublin. Maybe you know somebody. So I say, do you know this and this gentleman? She yes, yes. He is here with the kids with everybody. Here's the whole present and the Jewish Federation. I said, could you take me down to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, yes. It was in the evening, was not allowed to go. But she took me down. And I came down to Dabie, where's home already. So they take me. They gave me a bath, they brought me clothing, they brought me everything. And I was just home. The boys were my friends. And there I was staying not more than overnight. He was the president of the association, Jewish. He went-- he knew the police. He went down and told them I need a permit. But I should be able to go on the train home to Dabie. I know already my parents live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940. And he gave me money for a ticket to buy. And say, there was another Dabie near Warsaw. He said don't even give a permit to Dabie-- [NON-ENGLISH], they gave a permit only to Dabie. Because they wouldn't give [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't want the Jews to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got them to Dabie, and I came to the station. And I say, Dabie [NON-ENGLISH]. So they gave me a ticket, Dabie [NON-ENGLISH]. They didn't know anything. I paid. And they gave me a ticket. I came to Warsaw. In Warsaw, this train didn't go any farther. So as I went out on the station, they were staying the Polish Red Cross. And as I came right down, you coming from the army? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us all in. It was a few boys. So took us in. They gave us good accommodations. They gave us food, and warm, and warmer room, and to wash and everything. And the next day I went farther by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Red Cross know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They know, how the [INAUDIBLE], releasing the Jewish boys from the Polish army. They know that. All over Poland they know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you arrived in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't arrive so easy. I came by the border with the train, from Poland to Germany because my town Dabie was, at that time, Germany. So they stopped the trains and took everybody out. And so who was with the soldiers, ask how many Germans you shoot during the front, during the war. And they asked that question to nine Jewish boys. So they took us into the station. They took away the papers that we had that we are free from prison. They took away the tickets we have. They took everything away. And they put it in the station. They put on a-- a-- guards who watch us we shouldn't run away. So we're again in prison. There came in an officer, a German. And I speak German. So I went down to him. And I asked him. Listen, I say, officer, we're here. We don't know why we're here.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, where are you coming from? I say, we're coming from Germany from the war prison camp. And we're going on the way home. I say, why don't you go? Why do you have the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, the police took all the papers. They don't let us go any farther. We don't know why. So he say, I go into the police. And I find out. We could hear during the war, Jews, Jews, Jews. [LAUGHS] Then he came back and say-- this took me [INAUDIBLE], BUT it connects to fear. Anymore-- I can't help anything. And I say we have the families there. We have to go home. He said, don't worry about. Your family's going to come here to Poland too. So he said--
